serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char restaurant serving of salted egg yolk butter pork ribs is approximately 250g, comprising roughly 200g of deep-fried pork ribs tossed in a sauce made from 3 salted duck egg yolks, 15g butter, and 30ml evaporated milk with curry leaves and dried chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Yolk Butter Pork Ribs — Component calculation using USDA FoodData Central (pork ribs, duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk) and Singapore HPB frying absorption data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Nutrition Information
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Values derived by component calculation: USDA FDC SR Legacy entries for pork ribs (NDB 10124), duck egg yolk (NDB 01136), butter (NDB 01001), and evaporated milk (NDB 01085), combined with HPB Singapore frying oil absorption data (~8–10% fat uptake for deep-fried pork). Salted duck egg yolk sodium contribution estimated from Singapore HPB salted egg composition data. Weighted proportions: pork ribs 200g + sauce components 50g = 250g total portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Protein-Fat-Carbohydrate Dishes — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate mixed dishes with cornstarch-battered components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg yolk butter pork ribs. GI estimated at ~40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for high-protein, high-fat dishes with small cornstarch-batter carbohydrate components. The dominant macronutrient profile (protein 16g/100g, fat 22g/100g) substantially attenuates glycaemic response. Carbohydrate 7.5g/100g is primarily from cornstarch batter and evaporated milk lactose. GL per 250g serving = (40 × 18.75g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5 (LOW). Confidence: 0.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,800mg per 250g serving (90% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit), primarily from salted duck egg yolks (~700–800mg Na), pork rib seasoning (~600mg Na), and minor contributions from butter and evaporated milk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 250g serving delivers ~1,800mg sodium, representing 90% of the WHO daily sodium limit, and regular consumption will substantially elevate blood pressure in sodium-sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic load (GL ~7.5 per serving) is favourable, but very high caloric density (~745 kcal per 250g serving), high saturated fat (~25g per serving), and very low dietary fibre (~0.5g per serving) present significant metabolic concerns for weight management and insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the glycaemic load is low (GL ~7.5), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish only occasionally and in reduced portions, as the very high caloric density (~745 kcal), high saturated fat (~25g), and negligible fibre (0.5g) adversely affect weight management and long-term insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Triple cardiovascular risk burden: saturated fat ~25g per serving (125% of daily limit), dietary cholesterol ~712mg per serving (~3× the historical 300mg daily limit, primarily from 3 salted duck egg yolks), and sodium ~1,800mg per serving — all in a single dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; a single 250g serving delivers approximately 25g saturated fat (125% of daily limit), 712mg dietary cholesterol, and 1,800mg sodium, creating a compounded cardiovascular risk that is clinically unacceptable for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~25g/serving) from butter, egg yolk, and pork promotes systemic inflammation and LDL elevation; very high sodium (~1,800mg) may exacerbate inflammatory burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat content (~25g/serving), which promotes pro-inflammatory pathways, and its very high sodium load (~1,800mg/serving).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Yolk Butter Pork Ribs (Zi Char Style) is a signature Singapore zi char restaurant dish consisting of approximately 200g of deep-fried pork ribs tossed in a rich, creamy sauce made from 3 salted duck egg yolks, 15g butter, 30ml evaporated milk, fresh curry leaves, and dried chilli, yielding a standard portion of ~250g. The dish originated as part of the Singapore 'salted egg yolk craze' of approximately 2013–2015 and became a staple on zi char menus from 2016 onwards. The sauce — a Singapore culinary innovation — is characterised by its intensely savoury, umami-rich, slightly sandy texture from the cooked egg yolk solids, with aromatic notes from curry leaves and mild heat from dried chilli. Per 100g: 298 kcal, 16g protein, 22g fat (10g saturated), 7.5g carbohydrate, 720mg sodium, 285mg cholesterol. Per 250g serving: ~745 kcal, 40g protein, 55g fat (~25g saturated fat = 125% daily limit), 18.75g carbohydrate, ~1,800mg sodium (90% WHO daily limit), ~712mg dietary cholesterol (~3× historical daily limit). Glycaemic index ~40 (low); glycaemic load ~7.5 (low) — the only favourable metabolic metric. Clinically, this dish presents a triple cardiovascular risk burden: very high saturated fat, very high dietary cholesterol, and very high sodium in a single serving. Suitable only for occasional consumption by healthy individuals; contraindicated for regular consumption by those with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia. Source: USDA FoodData Central component calculation + Singapore HPB frying data. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Singapore zi char dish. Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (pork ribs, duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk) combined with Singapore HPB frying absorption data. The component proportions (200g pork ribs + 50g sauce) are based on the dish description and standard zi char portion conventions. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the calculation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite deep-fried dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; individual component fatty acid data could be summed in a future revision. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish after deep-frying and sauce coating is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g reported as 0.1g/100g from USDA FDC butter component data (trace naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant sources). All other per_100g fields are populated from component calculation. GI estimated at ~40 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GL per 250g serving calculated as (40 × 18.75g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5. Cholesterol per serving ~712mg is derived from 3 salted duck egg yolks (~200mg cholesterol each) plus pork rib contribution. Sodium per serving ~1,800mg is derived from salted duck egg yolks (~700–800mg), pork rib seasoning (~600mg), and minor sauce components. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input data appears incorrect for this dish type and has been reclassified to dinner/sharing meal context.
